questions.text: I tend to see helping others as secondary to achieving my own objectives.
My startle response is mild — unexpected events register but don't jolt me.
I often find that I help the group define what success looks like together.
I routinely say no to requests that don't align with my personal objectives.
I gravitate toward letting discussions continue until the group is satisfied.
I tend to work extra hours not for my own advancement, but to lighten the load for my team.
I prefer to own the outcome of a decision rather than share that ownership.
I think that the ability to evaluate situations of human suffering with precision requires deliberate emotional self-regulation.
I feel that relying on willpower is exhausting; I'd rather follow my natural energy.
I can engage with intense subject matter — war, loss, tragedy — without being emotionally flooded.
I do not pay much attention to facial expressions during conversations.
I hold the view that the consistency of my moral concern for suffering — not its peak intensity — is the truest measure of its depth.
I value variety in my daily routine because it keeps me mentally sharp and engaged.
I notice the difference between someone who is quiet because they are content and someone who is quiet because they are upset.
I find that I am at my most effective when I can engage with a problem without being emotionally invested in the people involved.
I find that writing out my goals makes me significantly more likely to achieve them.
My emotional landscape has extreme peaks and valleys rather than rolling hills.
I do not automatically read into people's tone or body language.
